ontology.foodex2_description: Blueberry jam — classified under fruit jams and preserves in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for blueberry jam as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed fruit spreads is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of blueberry jam is approximately 1 tablespoon (20 g), spread thinly on toast or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Blueberry Jam — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for blueberry jam (cr_id F10010001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Jam and Fruit Spreads — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 51 (low) for blueberry jam is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fruit jam. Blueberry jam is classified as a low-GI food due to the presence of fruit acids, pectin, and fructose which slow glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 11.21) / 100 = 5.7 per 20 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (52.83 g/100 g) in a solid food with low fibre (1.05 g/100 g), promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation despite low sodium (11 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit blueberry jam to no more than 1 teaspoon (5 g) per occasion due to its extreme sugar concentration (52.83 g/100 g), which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction; no-added-sugar alternatives are strongly preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 52.83 g/100 g far exceeds the diabetes hard gate of 20 g/100 g; despite a low GI of 51, the extreme sugar density and low fibre (1.05 g/100 g) produce significant postprandial glucose excursions even in small portions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blueberry jam is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (52.83 g/100 g); if used at all, restrict to a maximum of 1 teaspoon (5 g) and pair with high-fibre foods, or substitute with no-added-sugar jam sweetened with stevia or erythritol.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extremely high free sugar content (52.83 g/100 g) with negligible fat and very low fibre; chronic high free-sugar intake is independently associated with dyslipidaemia, elevated triglycerides, and increased cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of blueberry jam given its very high free sugar content (52.83 g/100 g), which raises triglycerides and promotes atherogenic dyslipidaemia; use should be restricted to occasional trace amounts (1 teaspoon/5 g) or replaced with no-added-sugar alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 52.83 g/100 g with fibre only 1.05 g/100 g strongly promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; processing degrades blueberry anthocyanins, eliminating meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid blueberry jam as its extreme sugar concentration (52.83 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation; fresh blueberries or no-added-sugar blueberry products are preferred to retain anthocyanin anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blueberry jam is a processed fruit spread made by cooking blueberries with a significant amount of sugar and pectin, often with added citric acid, until the mixture thickens into a gel-like consistency. It typically presents with a vibrant purple-blue color, a smooth to slightly chunky texture depending on the preparation, and a predominantly sweet flavor with a subtle tartness from the blueberries. This popular condiment is commonly spread on toast, bread, scones, or used as a filling for pastries, tarts, and cakes. It also serves as a topping for yogurt, pancakes, waffles, and ice cream. Nutritionally, blueberry jam is characterized by its very high sugar content (52.83 g per 100 g), which contributes significantly to its energy density (223 kcal per 100 g). It contains minimal protein (0.1 g/100 g) and very low fat (0.18 g/100 g), with a low dietary fibre content (1.05 g/100 g). The sodium content is also low at 11 mg/100 g. While fresh blueberries are known for their antioxidant-rich anthocyanins, the extensive heat processing and high sugar concentration involved in jam production can substantially reduce or degrade these beneficial compounds. From a clinical perspective, blueberry jam is generally considered unsuitable for individuals managing conditions such as Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its extreme free sugar load. Although it has a low glycemic index of 51, the high carbohydrate density means even small portions can lead to significant postprandial glucose spikes. Blueberry jam is typically vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for halal diets. It is best consumed sparingly, in very small amounts (e.g., 1 teaspoon or 5 g), as an occasional condiment rather than a regular dietary staple, with no-added-sugar alternatives being a healthier choice.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in blueberry jam?
Is blueberry jam good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of blueberry jam?
How many calories in blueberry jam?
Can I eat blueberry jam if I have heart disease?
What is a healthy alternative to blueberry jam?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10010001) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI value of 51 is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database for fruit jam as an analogous estimate. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a processed blueberry jam product.
notes: Saturated fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the near-zero total fat (0.18 g/100 g) of this product — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid subtype, and moisture data for blueberry jam are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this record. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 19297 — Jams and preserves) provides partial micronutrient data but was not used as the primary source given the HPB canonical record. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is the best available match for blueberry jam; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible code under the fruit jams and preserves hierarchy — reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code currency against the latest EFSA FoodEx2 release. GI source is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fruit jam; a direct blueberry-specific jam GI entry was not identified as of the access date. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
